text: ABI-DIZ, or Dizfu'l, a stream of Khuzistan, a tributary of the Karún, into which it flows, on the r. bank, at Bund-i-Kir, in N lat. 31° 49', opposite the junction of the southern extremity of the Abi-Gargar branch with the Karún.
ABI-ELWAND. See HOLWAN.

ABI-GARGAR, one of the two branches into which the Karún river divides itself immediately below the city of Shushter in Khúzistán. It passes Khasamābad, and rejoins the Karun at Bund-i-Kir, 30 m. below Shushter. It is in great part an artificial cut or canal, of nearly 50 m. in length, with 12 ft. of water, and a breadth of from 60 to 120 yards. It is supplied from the Karún by means of a bund or dam drawn across that river at Shushter, and is doubtless identical with the Nahri-Masrúkán of Abú-l-Tulá.— Selby and Layard, in Geog. Journ., vols. xiv. and xvi. ABI-GARM [i. e. Warm spring], the name of several small streams in Persia. 1. A river of Luristán which joins the Kerkhah in N lat. 32° 50'.—2. A stream and village in Irak, 90 m. NNE of Ispahan.—3. A stream and v. in Mazanderan, on the S shore of the Caspian sea, 20 m. NNW of Khunem-abad.
ABI-GOOM, a point in the Bolan pass in Beluchistan, 2,540 ft. above sea-level, where the stream of the Bolan disappears under ground.
ABIJRAS, a tribe of South American Indians, located between the Amazons and the Rio Napo, in the prov. of Minaes.
ABILA, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pashalik of Damascus, 21 m. ESE of Tabarieh, in 32° 43' N lat.
ABILD, a town of Denmark, in Sleswick, 5 m. NE of Tonder.
ABIMES (Les), or Quartel des Abimes, a town and cant. in the SW of Grand Terre, in Guadaloupe, on the Rivière Salée, 18 m. SW of Moule.

ABINEAU POINT, a promontory in the Niagara district of Upper Canada, 10 m. to the W of Fort Erie, projecting into Lake Erie. An extensive bay on each side of it affords good anchorage.—Abineau PORT, 13 m. WSW of Fort Erie, on the N side of Lake Erie.

ABINGDON, or Abington, a parliamentary burgh in Berkshire, on the Thames; 6 m. S of Oxford, and 56 WNW of London; 4 m. N of the Great Western railway, which sends off a branch to this place. The trade is considerable, in consequence of the facility of navigating the Thames with barges to London. The principal business of the town is malting. Some canvas and sail-cloth are also manufactured here. Pop. in 1801, 4,356; in 1841, 5,502. A. returns one M. P. Electors in 1837, 324; in 1842-3, 315. N lat. 51° 40′, W long. 1° 16′.
ABINGDON, the capital of Washington county in Virginia U.S. It is situated between the two forks of Holston river, 7 m. from each. Pop. 1,000.—Also a village in Harford co. Maryland. Pop. 120.
ABINGDON ISLAND, one of the Galápagos group, in the Pacific ocean, about 8 m. long by 4 broad. Its S point is in 0° 34' N lat., 90° 49' W.
ABINGER, a p. in Surrey. Acres 9,780. Pop. 920.
ABINGHALL, a p. in Gloucestershire. Acres 860. Pop. 239.
ABINGTON, a p. in Northamptonshire. Acres 1,190. Pop. 143.—Abington Great, a p. in Cambridgeshire. Acres 1,500. Pop. 358.—Abington
LITTLE, a p. in the same co. Acres 1,120. Pop. 277. -Abington-Pigotts, a p. in the same co. Acres 1,260. Pop. 232.
ABINGTON, a v. in the parish of Crawford-John, Lanarkshire, near the line of the Caledonian railway. Pop. 135.

ABINGTON, a town in Plymouth co. Massachusetts, U. S., 20 m. SSE of Boston. Pop. 3,214.— Also a town in Montgomery co. Pennsylvania, 149 m. W of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,704.—Also a town in Wayne co. Indiana. Pop. 923.—Also a town in Wyoming co. Pennsylvania, on Tunkhannock creek, 257 m. W of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,770. ABINTZY, ABINZI, or ABINSK TATARS, a people of Asiatic Russia, inhabiting the district of Kusnesk, in the gov. of Tomsk, about 600 m. ESE of Tobolsk. They call themselves Abahar, an appellation which seems to indicate a primitive people; for, in the Tatar language, aba—whence this name is derived— signifies 'father.' The A. formerly inhabited the banks of the Tom, near Kusnesk, and might be regarded as a branch of the Tomski Tatars; but, when the Téléoutes quitted the White Mountains to establish themselves in their present location, the Abintzys -who were the weaker party, but did not choose to be driven farther to the north—ascended the Tom to ABI the high mountains, on the N of the lake of Teletskoi, in which they now dwell. The A. are divided into several aimaks or tribes, but pay tribute for scarcely more than 100 bows. In appearance, character, manners, language, and dress, the Abintzys perfectly resemble the Téléoutes. Their occupations are the rearing of cattle, the chase, fishing, the smelting of iron, and agriculture.
ABION, a small island of Sweden, in the bay of Sundsvall.
ABIPI, a town of New Granada, in the dep. of Boyaca, on a branch of the Magdalena, 32 m. NE of Mariquita.

ABIPONES, a warlike tribe of South American Indians, located in the prov. of Chaco, near the centre of Paraguay, between the parallels of 28° and 30° S lat., on the banks of Rio de la Plata, consisting of about 5,000 persons. They employ themselves principally in hunting and fishing. Long lances, and arrows with iron points, are their weapons. Their females are not much darker than Spanish women. The men are tall, with aquiline noses; they are good swimmers, skilful horsemen, and fond of painting figures on their skin. Their caziques are, in times of peace, their judges; in war, their leaders. In peace, however, their authority is very limited. There are three families or clans of the Abipones. About the beginning of last century, having been defeated by the Mokoby, Mocobs, or Mocobios, a neighbouring tribe, they removed eastward, and placed themselves under Spanish protection. Martin Dobrizhoffer, a Jesuit missionary, who spent several years amongst this tribe in the middle of last century, has written a curious account of this people, of which a translation by Mr. Southey was published in 1821 in 3 vols. 8vo. -Malte Brun, vol. xv. p. 253 .- Quarterly Review, vol. xxvi. pp. 277-323.
ABI'RA, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Kerman, 68 m. ESE of Kerman.—Also a name given by early geographers to a tribe in Afghanistan in the neighbourhood of the modern Gundawa.
ABISCA, a district of Peru, to the E of the Andes, and the S of Cuzco, between the Yetan and Amarumain rivers.

ABISCOUN. See ABESGHUN. ABI-SHIRIN [i. e. Sweet or Fresh river], a "broad and noble stream," which, rising in the mountains of the Mamesenni, flows in a SW course to join the Abi-Shur. It is sometimes called the Tab; and De Bode conjectures it may have been the Arosis of the ancients. ABI-SHUR, [i. e. Salt river] or She'ker-ab, a considerable stream in the Persian prov. of Fars, which, descending from Ardekán, waters the beautiful valley of Sha'b-Beván or Bawan, in a winding course from E to W; and, forcing its way through the hills to the SW of Fahliyan, joins the Ab-Shirin coming from the SW near Galab. The united streams, assuming the name of the Zohreh, a river of Hindiyan, discharge their waters into the Persian gulf a few miles below the village of Hindiyan. It is navigable up to Zeitun, within 18 m. of Behbehán. -De Bode and Layard. ABI-SIAH [i. e. Black water], a small headstream of the Helmund in Afghanistan, descending from the S side of the Hajikak, in the Huzara country. - Wood.

ABISO, or Abisso, a river of Sicily, which rising in Mount Laura, near Palazzuolo, and, under the name of the Atellara, flowing through the valley of Noto, falls into the Ionian sea 10 m. N of Cape Passaro.

ABISTA'DEH, a lake of Kabul into which the Pultsí and several other streams flow. It is in N lat. 32° 6', E long. 67° 7'; and has an elevation of at least 7,000 ft. Masson says its waters are salt, and that they extended "as far as the eye could reach to | the south." [Journeys, vol. i. p. 262.] Outram estimates its diameter to be about 12 m. It probably varies in its dimensions according to the season.

ABI-TALH, or ALA'R, a river of Luristán, rising in or perhaps beyond the Munghasht mountains, running from E to W through the rich valley of Mei- Dawud [Acacia water]; and then turning S, and receiving four affluents from the E, reaches the plain of Ram Hormuz, where, after a course of 40 m., it falls into the Jerahi or Kurdistán.—De Bode and Layard in Geog. Journ., vols. xiii. and xvi.
ABITIBBE. See ABBITIBBE.
ABIUL, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, 21 m. NNW of Thomar.
ABIVERD, or BAVERD, a v. of Persia, near Killaat, 30 m. NE of Meshed, celebrated as the birthplace, in 1688, of Nadir Shah.

ABIYAD, the principal place of Tembien in Abyssinia. It is in N lat. 13° 40', and about 34 m. NW of Antalo. ABI-ZARD [i. e. Yellow river], a river descending from the Mungasht mountains, near the village of Malágai, into the plain of Bághi-Malek, after flowing through which, and forcing its way through a precipitous limestone ridge to the E of Manjanik, it joins the Tala and Abi Ram Hormuz.—De Bode.
ABI-ZAL, a river of Susiana. See AB-KUREN.
ABJAC DE MONTRON, a v. of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, 7 m. NE of Montron. Pop. 1,233.
AB-KETTLEBY, a p. and town in Leicestershire, 31 m. NW of Melton-Mowbray. Acres 2,920. Pop. 380.

ABKHANA, a ferry on the Cabul river, on the route from Peshawer to Cabul, in N lat. 34° 16', at a point where the river presents a frightful scene of rocks, whirlpools, and surges, but through all which dangers passengers are safely guided upon skin-floats guided and impelled by paddlers and swimmers.— Masson. ABKHASSES, ABKHASETI, ABKHASSIANS. See ABASIA.

AB-KUREN, or KARU'N, or KUR-AB, the largest river of Farsistan, or, perhaps, in respect of volume of water, of all Persia. It is formed by the union of two main branches. One of these, coming from the NW, is generally supposed to be the Abi-Zal or river of Dizful, which rises in the mountains to the NW of Morgab, and at the village of Sowan, 20 m. NE of the Takhti-Jamshid, divides into two arms,— the one still retaining its original name and running W to the Araxes or Bendemis, and the other running SE, under the name of the Sowan, till it unites with the combined stream of the Ab-Kuren, at the village of Bund-i-Kír. Its length of course is about 130 m. The Araxes, or NE branch of the Ab-Kuren, rises at the foot of the Kúh-i-Zard, 70 m. SW of Ispahan; flows through the Bachtiyari range, emerges from the hills 3 m. NE of Shushter, flows past Shushter in N lat. 32°, where it is crossed by a bridge of 9 arches, erected on a bund or dam thrown across the river, and where the Abi-Gargar offset or canal leaves it to reunite with it at Bund-i-Kir; and after a course of about 170 m. from its source, unites with the Abi-Zal, or river of Dizful, a few miles below Shushter, and 10 m. above Wais. The combined stream is larger than the Tigris, or the Euphrates, taken separately; and after a SSW course of 180 m., in which it passes the towns of Wais and Ahwaz, disunites at Sablah, 12 m. below the extreme tidal point, and enters the sea by several mouths forming a delta 65 m. in length, by 40 m. of medial breadth, inhabited by the Cha'b Arabs. Of these mouths the Khór-Bámushir, the most western, and perhaps the principal, is about 40 m. in length and ½ m. in width; runs interior is generally flat; but near the coasts, and ABO parallel with that mouth of the Euphrates known as the Shat-al-Arab, or river of Basrah, is distant from it only 3 m., and is in fact connected with it by a haffar or canal, about 12 m. distant from the Persian gulf, through which it is said to discharge the greater part of its waters into the Shat-al-Arab. In fact this channel appears at ebb-tide to receive scarcely any water from the Karun. This haffar is about \( \frac{3}{4} \) m. in length and from 200 to 400 yards in breadth, and has a depth of from 30 to 42 ft. On either side of it, and half-way between the two streams, is the town of Mohammerah, an important commercial mart, which is under the Arab sheik of Gaban, but standing as it does on the line which divides Persia from the pashalik of Baghdad affords a constant bone of contention to these powers. From below Ahwaz the Karun flows through a vast alluvial and uncultivated plain, thinly sprinkled with trees, and inhabited chiefly by jackals and wild boars. The Jerahi, a considerable river descending from the mountains of Luristan, after passing Fellahiyah or Dorak, joins the eastern mouth of the Karun, about 20 m. above its outfall into the gulf.—Bell's Geog., vol. iii. p. 427.—Selby in Geog. Journ., vol. xiv. -Ainsworth.
ABLA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Granada, on the r. bank of the Rio de Almeria, and about 12 m. from the source of that river.
ABLA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Acre, in the plain between the Libanus and Anti- Libanus, in N lat. 33° 53'.

ABLACH, a river of Würtemberg, which rises to the S of Sendenhard in Hohenzollern, and after forming a junction with the Andelsbach from the S, pursues a NE course to the Danube, which it joins on the r. bank, to the N of Mengen.—There is a village of the same name on its banks.

ABLAIKIT, a ruined town of Tartary, in N lat. 49° 20′, and E long. 83° 5′, at the foot of a range of mountains rising to the height of 3,000 feet above this place, and known as the Ablaikit mountains.
ABLASSERDAM, or Alblasserdam, a town of Holland, in the prov. of S Holland, 4 m. N of Dort.
ABLAWA, a Bulgarian village on the l. bank of the Kara-Lom, 30 m. SE of Sistov.
ABLIS, a village of France, in the dep. of Seineet-Oise, 7 m. W from Dourdan. Pop. 800.
ABLITAS, a village of Spain, in the prov. of Pamplona, on a stream flowing into the lake del Santo, 4 m. S of Tudela. Pop. 1,240. Lat. 42° N.
ABLON, a town of France, in the dep. of Seineet-Oise, on the banks of the Seine, 9 m. SSE of Paris.
ABLONITZA, a town of Bulgaria, 30 m. SW of Lofitscha, near the r. bank of the Isker.
ABLORIALIK BAY, a bay on the N coast of Labrador, in N lat. 59° 45′, and 65° 20′ W long., on the NE side of Ungava or South bay, in Hudson's strait.

ABO, a loen or circle of the gov. of Finland in European Russia. It comprehends Finland proper, a small part of Easter Bothnia, and all the group of the Aland isles; and has a superficies of nearly 12,000 sq. m. It extends from 59° 50' to 62° 20' N lat., and from 19° 20' to 24° 4' E long. It is bounded on the N by the district of Vasa; on the E by that of Helsingfors; on the S by the gulf of Finland; and on the W by the gulf of Bothnia, in which is the group of the Aland isles. In 1805 it had 207,960 inhabitants; and contained 6 towns and 4,980 farms or holdings. In 1835 its pop. was 250,238. It is divided into 9 districts named justiciaries; viz., Rikie, Masko, Virmo, Vems, Satakunda Upper, Satakunda Lower, Central Satakunda, Aland, and Haliko. This district takes its name from that of its capital. The especially towards the S, there are some mountains. There are several lakes, among which is the Pyhæiærvi, situated nearly in the centre. The cataracts of the Kumoioki are remarkable, and limit the navigation of that river. All the coasts from the S to the SE and SW are studded with small islands, some inhabited, others barren and desert. The soil of the district is fertile, especially to the S, where it is also well-cultivated, and produces grain, cabbage, potatoes, lint, hemp, tobacco, and hops. Much attention is paid to the rearing of cattle. In the N and E are extensive forests which furnish wood for building, tar, rosin, and potash. The coasts, as well as the lakes and rivers, abound in fish. The mountains furnish lime and slate; and the marshes iron-ore, but in small quantities. The inhabitants, who are princicipally Swedes, are an industrious race. ABO [FINNISH, Turku]—pronounced Obo,—a town and port of European Russia, the capital, until 1817, of the gov. of Finland. It is situated on the Aurajoki, or Aeura, at the extremity of the isthmus formed by the gulfs of Finland and Bothnia, in N lat. 60° 27′, E long. 22° 17½′; 330 m. S by W from Tornea, and 235 m. WNW of St. Petersburg. It is surrounded on all sides by bare rocky hills chiefly of granitic formation. Before its last devastation by fire in 1827, it contained 3 public places or squares, and 102 streets, chiefly composed of wooden houses. Its cathedral, built in 1300, and which has almost miraculously survived the various disasters which have fallen upon the city, is 300 ft. in length, 127 ft. in breadth, and 150 ft. in height. The interior is in the Gothic style, but the outside exhibits a pile of plain brickwork. It was the seat of the supreme court-of-justice for S Finland, and of a Lutheran archbishopric. Gustavus Adolphus founded here, in 1626, a college, which was erected by Queen Christina into a university in 1640. And Clarke says, "its men of letters would have done honour to any seat of science." In 1824 it had 40 professors, and 500 students: the average number in later years was 300. The buildings occupied a beautiful site; and contained a library of 35,000 volumes, a collection of philosophical instruments, a cabinet of natural history, and an observatory. But the glory of A. has departed since the erection of Helsingfors into the capital of Russian Finland, and the translation of the university to that place. The pop. of A. in 1791 was 8,504; in 1827, 14,000; it is now about 15,000.— A. conducts a considerable trade with the interior of Finland, and has manufactures of cloth, hardware, soap, sugar, tobacco, and silks; some tanneries, saw-mills, two ship-building-yards, and a manufactory of glass. The mouth of the Aurajoki forms the harbour of A., from which a foreign trade is conducted with French, Spanish, and Italian ports. Only small vessels can ascend the river to the quay; those of large burthen anchor 3 m. SE from the town. The harbour is defended by the fort of Abo-Slot or Abohus. Above 40 vessels belonging to this port are engaged in fishing, and in the coasting and foreign trade. A. exports annually above 65,000 fir deals, and 700 tons of tar, some corn and other commodities, besides manufactured articles.—It has suffered much from fires, and especially from that of 1775; and still more from that of 1827 when 780 houses, together with the university buildings and the library, were destroyed. Clarke declares its history from 1198, when it was first consumed by fire, to be "nothing more than a catalogue of disasters, conflagrations, and catastrophes of every description."-On the 17th of August 1743, Russia and Sweden concluded a treaty of peace here, in which Sweden, besides formally renewing her cession of Ingria, Livonia, and Esthonia, cities and fortresses of Fredericshamm and Wilmanstrand, and the city and port of Nyslot; while Russia restored Abo, Biorneborg, and East Bothnia. From that time the river Kymmené formed the boundary with Russia, until 1809 when the latter power obtained the whole of Finland.

ABODI, a v. in the territory of Aquapim, on the Gold coast of Africa, 8 m. N of Christiansberg. ABO'H, IBO, or EBOE, the principal town of a territory of the same name, forming the most western part of the Ibo country. It is situated near the apex of the delta of the Niger, 130 m. from the mouth of that river, and 95 m. below Iddah; in about 5° 50' N The Niger below this town varies from 500 to lat. 1,000 yards in breadth; above, it expands into the form of a lake thickly studded with islands, and in some places 2 m. in width, which lake-like appearance it preserves for nearly 50 or 60 m. up the stream. The Bonny branch of the Niger runs off to the SE a few miles above Aboh; the Benin branch flowing to the SW separates from the main stream on the r. bank at Anya 20 m. below Aboh. The Benin people come up as far as Aboh for palm-oil, which they pay for in rum, gunpowder, and muskets.—Parl. Paper, 1843.—Allen in Admiralty Chart of Quorra.
ABOITE, a town of Indiana, U. S., 120 m. NNE of Indianapelis. Pop. 235.
ABOKNA, a town of Sennár, 100 m. ESE of Sennár, in Nubia, on the road to Gondar. Lat. 13° N.

ABO'MEY, or AGBO'ME, the capital of the kingdom of Dahomy, on the Slave coast of Africa; in N lat. 7° 27', E long. 2° 20'; about 80 m. N by W from Whydah. Pop. 24,000. The route to this town lies through a fertile open country, in some parts thickly wooded, in others covered with high grass, or under cultivation, and producing pease, maize, and yams. It passes through the towns of Xavier or Sabi, and Tori.
ABO'MSA, a town of Abyssinia, in the prov. of Efat, to the W of Makhfud. Lat. 9° 57' N.
ABONA (PORT), a small port on the E coast of the isle of Teneriffe.
ABONDANCE, a town of Savoy, on the Dranse, 10 m. S of the lake of Geneva. Pop. 1,800.
ABORIGINES, a term used by modern geographers to denote the primitive or earliest inhabitants of any country, as distinguished from succeeding settlers or colonists.
ABORS, a rude tribe inhabiting the high lands of Assam, and the southern exposure of the Himala chain, in about N lat. 28° and between E long. 93° and 94°.

ABORIMA, one of the Samoa group in the South Seas. It lies half-way between Manono and Savayi. Its name—which signifies 'Hollow of the hand'—indicates its general configuration, which in the interior is that of a regularly sloping basin covered with rich vegetation, 472 ft. high, lat. 13° 49' S, long. 172° 3' W.

ABORLAN, a seaport on the E coast of the island of Palawan, in N lat. 9° 30', E long. 119° 50'. ABOUKIR, ABUKI'R, or NELSON'S ISLAND, an island of the Mediterranean, on the coast of Egypt, within a league to the NE of the v. of the same name. The ancient remains which have been found on this island prove that a great part of its original extent has been submerged by the sea. The wreck of the French ship L'Orient lies about 25 m. to the NW. The reef on which the Culloden struck in going into the battle of the Nile, extends half-aleague to the NE. [Yates.] After that action, this island became a complete charnel-house where our sailors raised rude mounds of sand over the heaps of dead.—Clarke. ABOUKIR-pronounced Abúkéer-a v. of Lower Egypt, in the prov. of Baheirah, situated on a promontory or neck of land running out between Lake ceded to Russia the prov. of Kymmenégard, with the | Mareotis and the sea, about 13 m. NE of Alexandria, in N lat. 31° 20½', Elong. 30° 6'. According to some authors, A. is built on the ruins of the ancient Canopus, where the Egyptians worshipped Serapis under the form of a rotund vase surmounted with a human head. The temple of this idol was destroyed by Theophilus, patriarch of Alexandria, who founded a monastery in its place. Others suppose that the ruins and rock-cut temples which are to be seen here belonged to the ancient Taposiris. But it is not improbable that many of them are a portion of the 400 columns of granite which Caradjah, governor of Alexandria, by the command of Saladin ordered to be thrown into the sea to prevent the vessels of the Crusaders from approaching the coast. The castle of Aboukir to the NE of the village is used as a state-prison by the pasha. — The bay (from which it has taken its name) is enclosed on the SW by the tongue of land on which the town stands, supposed to be the Zephyrium promontory of Strabo; and on the NE by the boghaz of Rosetta, that is, the point at the eastern mouth of the Nile. On the E side of the bay there are 8 or 9 fathoms water; but it is not sufficiently sheltered to afford safe anchorage for large vessels-of-war during the stormy seasons; and the whole of the W side aboun ds in rocks and shoals. The position and outline of A. bay will be seen in the map of the coast and environs of Alexandria given in the present work: the subjoined sketch of the land around the bay, and bird's-eye view of the village, is from Denon. This place has become distinguished in modern times, by the great naval battle in which Nelson annihilated the French fleet, between the 1st and 3d of August, 1798. The French fleet lay moored in the form of a crescent, as near as possible to the island of A., and protected towards the W by a battery of cannon and mortars placed upon it. Nelson directed half of his force to break through between the island and the French line-of-battle, and to sail under the shore in their rear; while the other half approached their front, and anchored within pistol-shot; thus doubling on the French line, so that the French ships were attacked at once on both sides. At sunset, about halfpast 6 o'clock, the battle began; and at the end of an hour, 5 French ships were dismasted and taken. Only 2 ships-of-the-line and 2 frigates escaped, to be captured soon afterwards; 9 ships-of-the-line were taken; 1 blown up; another frigate was burned by the French themselves; and one was sunk. In this—the battle of the Nile, as it is usually called—the naval power of France in the Mediterranean was a second time annihilated, and the British flag waved triumphant from Gibraltar to Alexandria. On the 8th of March, 1801, Sir Ralph Abercrombie landed near A., in the face of a French army which was drawn up within gun-shot of Aboukir castle; and on the 21st the ABR memorable battle was fought in which Abercrombie conquered and fell.

ABOYNE, an extensive and mountainous p. in Aberdeenshire, having an area of about 29,000 acres, whereof not more than 3,000 are arable. It is intersected by the upper waters of the Dee, and embraces the district of Glentanner once celebrated for its pine forests. Pop. in 1801, 916; in 1841, 1,138. Houses in 1841, 239. Its only v. is Charlestown.
ABQUOULQUI, a small town of Sennaar, in N lat. 10° 39', E long. 34° 53'.
ABRA, a town of Sennaar, between the Dender and Rohad rivers; 70 m. ESE of Sennaar.
ABRA, a town of Upper Guinea, in the kingdom of Fantee, 18 m. from the coast.
ABRA, a deep inlet or bay of Long Reach, in the strait of Magalhaens. Its mouth is about 11 m. across, with an islet in the entrance.
ABRA-PAMPA, a small town of Bolivia, on the eastern ascent of the Cordillera del Despoblado; in S lat. 22° 10′.
ABRAHAM'S DORF, a town of Upper Hungary in the county of Zips. Pop. 2,500.
ABRAHAM'S ISLAND, or Isle Maria, a small island on the NE coast of Madagascar, in lat. 17° N.

ABRAHAM (Plains of), the name given to the level summit of the promontory on the l. bank of the St. Lawrence, formed by the confluence of the St. Charles river with the St. Lawrence, at the NE extremity of which, known as Cape Diamond, the citadel of Quebec stands. Their altitude above the river level is 330 ft.-It was on these plains that General Wolfe, having scaled the precipitous banks of the St. Lawrence during the night, defeated the French under Montcalm, and fell in the moment of victory, on the 13th of September 1759.
ABRAHAS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, 10 m. N by W of Santarem.
ABRAM, a township in the p. of Wigan, Lancashire. Pop. 901. The v. is 43 m. NNE of Newton.
ABRAM'S CREEK, a small stream of New York, U. S., which falls into the Hudson, 4 m. above Hudson.
ABRANTES, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, on the l. bank of the Joannes, 3 m. from the sea, and 20 NE of the capital; in S lat. 12° 52', W long. 33° 17′.

ABRANTES, a fortified town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura near the r. bank of the Tagus, 75 m. direct distance NE of Lisbon; in N lat. 39° 26', W long. 8° 15'. Pop. 5,000. Its church of San Vincente is reckoned the finest in Portugal. A. is built on a rising ground covered with orchards, while the plain to the E produces melons, pumpkins, and other vegetables, which are transported in large quantities by the river for the supply of the capital. During the peninsular war, A. was of great importance as a military position. In 1808 the French army under Junot arrived at A. after a dangerous march through Beira along the banks of the Tagus. Junot immediately garrisoned the castle and town, and directed the works to be strengthened, while, with his fatigued troops he pushed on to Lisbon, which he entered and took possession of at the head of only 1,500 grenadiers. For this gallant action he was created Duke of Abrantes by Napoleon. A. afterwards fell into the hands of Wellington, who added considerably to its defences.
ABRECHWEILLER, a town of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, 6 m. SSE of Sarreburg. Pop. 1,600. There are iron mines in the neighbourhood.
ABREGA, a town of Illyria, N of Rovigno.
ABREIRO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, 24 m. E of Villa-Real. Pop. 250.

ABREOJOS, or Abrojos, a reef or shoal in the Bahama group, on the SE of Turk's island, near the Handkerchief and Silver Cay passages; in N lat. 20° 53' to 21° 23', W long. 70° 24' to 70° 55'.—Also a rock or reef off Abreojos point, on the W coast of Lower California, in N lat. 26° 40'.—The name is the same as the Portuguese Abrolhos: which see.
ABRIES, a town of France, in the dep. of Hautes Alpes, 15 m. SE of Briançon. Pop. 2,033.
ABRIOLA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, 9 m. S of Potenza. Pop. 3,000.
ABRO, a small islet of Russia, off the coast of Livonia, 3 m. S of Arensburg.

ABROLHOS, or SANTA BARBARA ISLES, a group of 4 islets, 12 m. in extent, lying 40 m. off Villa Vicoza, on the coast of Brazil; the E summit of the N island, 122 ft. high, is in lat. 18° 5' S, long. 38° 42' W; the NW islet is 138 ft. high. The islands are barren, but abound in marine birds, and the seas around in the fish called garupa. The Abrolhos bank is a well known shoal extending 86 m. from the islands, and 126 m. from the coast of Brazil, to its eastern limit, in long. 37° 6' W. Some parts of the bank are very irregular, the depths varying from 20 to 50 fath. There is a channel 9 m. wide between the islets and the shoals, called Parcel das Paredes, to the W, with a depth of 10 fath. water.—Roussin Pilote du Brásil, 1845.—Fitzroy Voy. of the Beagle, 1839.—Raper, 3d Ed.

ABROLHOS, a group, or rather cluster of groups of coral islands, off the W coast of Australia, from which they are separated by the Gelvink channel. Commander Stokes places the NE point of Rat island near the centre of the group, in S lat. 32° 43' and E long. 1° 58' W of Swan river. They extend in a NNW direction 48 m. and appear to rest on the outer extremity of a bank stretching out from the adjacent continent. On Gun island, forming the NW extremity of the Pelsart group in this cluster, some remains of the Zeewyk, wrecked in 1727, were discovered by the Beagle's boats in 1840. ABRUDBA'NYA [Germ. ALTENBURG], a district of Transylvania, lying along the Abrud, a branch of the Aranyos, in N lat. 46° 14'. It is a metalliferous region, and the Romans are supposed to have wrought both gold and silver mines here. It appears to retain traces of its ancient character. The mountaineers still find gold dust in the streams about it, and various pieces of rock containing gold are occasionally picked up. These are collected, and brought into A. every Monday from all quarters; then broken into pieces with a hammer, the metal extracted and its quantity ascertained, and an equivalent given for it. [Foreign Review, vol. xxviii. p. 187.]—The chief town, of the same name, is 25 m. WNW of Carlsburg. Banya in Hungarian signifies 'a mine.'

ABRUZZO, one of the great divisions of the continental part of the kingdom of Naples; now, with somewhat altered boundaries, divided into three provinces, viz.: - ABRUZZO ULTERIORE, or ULTRA [i. e. the Farther Abruzzo], which comprises two provinces distinguished as ABRUZZO ULTERIORE PRIMO, and ABRUZZO ULTERIORE SECUNDO; and ABRUZZO CITERIORE or CITRA [i. e. the Nearer Abruzzo]. The epithets Ulteriore and Citeriore are used to indicate the respective positions of the provinces to Naples the capital. Its superficial area is 3,972 sq. m. of 60 to the degree. Pop. in 1831, 735,931; in 1843, 817,608.—The etymology of the name of this central region of Italy has been the subject of much controversy. The general opinion inclines to its derivation from Aprutium, a name probably borne by the capital of the Prætutii, who, with the Marrucini, the Amiternini, the Marsi, the Vestini, and the Hirpini, occupied the district in early times. Towards the end of the 6th cent. Antharis, king of the Lombards, ABR bestowed the Abruzzo on the duke of Benevento, who established a chatelain for the government of the district at Teramo. With the rest of the duchy A. passed into the power of the Normans. Frederic II. made Sulmona the seat of a justiciary. Charles I. of Anjou, or, according to some, Alphonso I. of Aragon, divided A. into two provinces, lying respectively on the right and left of the Pescara. In 1684, Charles IV. divided A. into the three provinces of Aquila, Chieti, and Teramo, which in 1806 respectively received the appellations they now bear, viz., A. Ultra Secundo, A. Citra, and A. Ultra Primo. The civil administration of A. is conducted by an intendant-general who resides in the capital, and by sub-intendants in the different districts. Some communes, especially in A. Ultra Primo, have a particular government, and choose their own syndics. Each prov. has its own civil and criminal court; from all of which an appeal lies to the high civil court at Aquila. In ecclesiastical affairs, A. Ultra Primo is divided into the two dioceses of Teramo, and of Penne-et-Atri. A. Ultra Secundo comprises the dioceses of Aquila, Valvi, and Marsi. A. Citra is divided into the dioceses of Chieti and Lanciano. The fullest and most accurate description of A. will be found in the 2d vol. of a work entitled, 'Descrizione topografica fisica economica politica de' reali Dominij al di quà del Faro nel Regno delle due Sicilie etc. di Giuseppe del Re.' Napoli 1835. ABRUZZO CITRA is bounded on the NW by Abruzzo Ultra Primo; on the NE by the Adriatic; on the S by the prov. of Molise or Samno; and on the SW by Abruzzo Ulteriore Secundo. Its superficial area is 1,088 Italian sq. m. of 60 to the degree. Several branches of the Apennines cross the prov. in a SE to NW direction towards the Adriatic. Their highest points are the Prata, the Morrone, the Capraro and the Policorno; the soil is generally sandy and unproductive. The principal rivers are the Pescara, which forms the boundary with Abruzzo Ultra Primo; the Sango, which pursues a central course; and the Trigno, which to a considerable extent divides it from the prov. of Molise. These, and three small rivers, the Lenti, the Sente, and the Asinelle, all pursue a NE course and fall into the Adriatic. The climate is delightful; but in summer the heat of the sun is excessive; rain rarely falls, and the prevalence of drought increases the sterility of the soil. The natives cultivate wheat, maize, lint, and tobacco. Liquorice, fruits, wine, oil, and silks are likewise produced in abundance. The oil of Vasto and Francavilla is excellent; that of Bachianico and Ripa de Chieti is also good. This prov. has neither wood nor minerals. There are some manufactures of cloth; but the only exports are the natural products of the country, which are sent to Foggia. Though there is an extensive coast there is no harbour; the mouth of the Pescara is only navigable by small boats. In 1793 the pop. of this prov. amounted to 222,773. In 1831 it was returned at 265,474; in 1843, at 297,876 It is divided into three districts, viz.: Chieti on the N; Lanciano in the centre; and Il Vasto on the S. These are again subdivided into 25 bailliages, and 120 communes. Chieti, the finest town in all Abruzzo, is the capital town. The other towns are: Lanciano, Ortona a Mare, Pescara, and Vasto. ABRUZZO ULTRA PRIMO is bounded on the NW by the States-of-the-Church; on the NE by the Adriatic; on the SE by Abruzzo Citra; and on the SW by Abruzzo Ultra Secundo. Its superficial extent is 976 sq. m. of 60 to a degree. Pop. in 1793, 157,715; in 1831, 190,916; in 1843, 212,405. Towards the west it is exceedingly mountainous; and the boundary which separates it in that direction from Abruzzo Ultra Sehaving conquered a great portion of Southern Italy, | cundo is formed by one of the highest ridges of the Apennines. Numerous chains diverge from this main mountain range and cross the prov. in the direction of the Adriatic. The Colle Biferno, the Monte Castelli, and the St. Georgio are the most important summits. There are no plains, but many rich and extensive valleys watered by rivers which become torrents in the months of March and April but are nearly dry in summer. The Pescara forms the boundary between this province and Abruzzo Citra, and for a short distance the Tronto separates it from the States-of-the- Church. The climate is temperate; and the air pure and salubrious. On the coast, rains are more frequent than in the other provinces contiguous to the Adriatic, and form one of the principal sources of its fertility; but the inhabitants have little agricultural skill or industry. Whole districts are covered with brambles, thorns, and ferns; irrigation is unknown, and the unbanked torrents are suffered to devastate the finest meadows. Lint, tobacco, and the vine thrive well; some grain is exported, and wine forms an article of commerce. The high mountains are covered with forests of oak and pines. The rearing of cattle is a favourite occupation. The fisheries are considerable; and game and fowls are abundant. There are beds of magnesia, lime, gypsum, and marble; and indications of the existence of metals. A china manufactory at Castella is the only manufacturing establishment in the country. Teramo is the principal entrepôt of commerce.—The prov. of which Teramo is the capital is divided into two districts: Teramo, and Civita di Penne, which are subdivided into 17 bailliages, and 73 communes. The towns next in importance to the capital are: Campli, Penne, Civitella del Tronto, and Senarica. ABRUZZO ULTRA SECUNDO is bounded on the W and N by the States-of-the-Church; on the E by Abruzzo Ultra Primo; on the SE by Abruzzo Citra and the province of Molise or Sannio; and on the S by that of the Terra di Lavoro. Its superficial area is 1,908 sq. m. of 60 to a degree. Pop. in 1807, 251,500; in 1831, 279,541; in 1843, 307,327. The chain of the Apennines penetrates it on the NE. Its principal ridges divide into two branches on the frontiers of the States-of-the-Church. One of these branches runs in a NE direction, and forms the boundary with Abruzzo Ultra Primo; the other, taking a S course towards the lake Fucino, enters the Terra di Lavoro. From the main range—whose summits are covered with perpetual snow-diverge many branches, which, running in all directions, cover the surface of the province with mountains of various dimensions. The highest of these are the Lampello Grottolo, Turchio, Carbonaro, Accerello, Torea, Carosa, Colle Candido, Natella, Velino, Peschiolo della Duchessa, Corbaro, and Luco, all situated in the vicinity of Lake Fucino. To the NE of Aquila, the Great Rock of Italy [Il Gran Sasso d'Italia], the highest point of the Apennines, attains an elevation of 9,521 ft. Here also rise the Ocra, the Colli di Corni, Col di Lavarete, and Col di Vigliano; and on the frontiers of the States-of-the- Church, the Sassuolo, Taglia, Purguri, and Roseto. The valleys, which are extensive, possess considerable fertility. The principal river, the Aterno, rises in the Col di Vigliano; flows to the S through the long valleys of Aquila and the valley of Putrida; then turning to the NE, quits the province near Tocco, and takes the name of Pescara. The Sangro, Ganigliano, Satto, Velino, and Tronto, take their rise in this province. None of these rivers are navigable, although their depth is often considerable. The largest lake in the kingdom, the Celano, or Fucinothe Fucinus of Roman times—is in this province. Its greatest length is 15 m.; average breadth, 5 m. It receives three small rivers, has no outlet, and often occasions disastrous inundations. The other lakes | Parde. Pop. 316. by Google ABT are of little importance. There are some mineral springs, but they are of small repute. The climate is temperate, pure, and salubrious. The Apennines here are covered with snow from October to April; the highest summits, all the year. The mountainous character of the country renders its culture very difficult; but in the valleys, grain, especially rye and maize, is raised in sufficient quantity for the wants of the inhabitants. Lint, hemp, and saffron-which is esteemed the best in Europe—are likewise cultivated. The wine is bad; the figs and almonds are excellent; the olive grows only in sheltered situations. Forests of excellent timber cover the mountains, and the rearing of cattle forms the principal occupation of the inhabitants; vast flocks of sheep are brought from the Capitanata, and other provinces to the S, to be pastured during summer on the upper regions of the mountains. The Merino breed has recently been introduced. The cheese which is made near Aquila is preferred by many to Parmesan. Game and fowls abound. Marble, clay, gypsum, and building-stones, are quarried. The only manufactures are lace, delf-ware, and paper. Aquila is the centre of the commerce; but the roads are neglected; communication with the neighbouring provinces is very difficult; and the poor inhabitant is often obliged, for the means of subsistence, to migrate to the adjacent States-of-the-Church as an agricultural labourer. The inhabitants of this prov. are almost all mountaineers, and distinguished for strength and activity. Sir R. C. Hoare praises their "genuine and cordial hospitality." Their dialect is considered the most elegant of the kingdom of Naples.—This province was formerly the country of the Marses, a warlike people. Its chief town is Aquila, on the Aterno or Pescara. Other towns are Avezzano, Capestrello, Angizia, Civita-Ducale, Pescina, and Sulmona. It is divided into the four districts of Aquila on the N; Civita-Ducale on the NW; Avezzano on the SW; and Sulmona on the SE. These are subdivided into 34 bailliages, and 123 communes.—Swinburne's Travels, vol. iv.—Classical Tour by Sir R. C. Hoare, 4to., 1819.—Craven's Excursions in the Abruzzi, 2 vols. 8vo., 1838.—Dictionnaire Geographique Universel.— Descrizione Topografica, &c., del Regno delle due Sicilie Napoli, 1835.—Almanacco reale del Regno delle due Sicilie per l'anno 1844.
ABSAL. See ABI-ZAL.
ABSBERG, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Mittel Franken, 6 m. ENE of Gunzenhausen. Pop. 600.
ABSDORF, two towns in the archduchy of Austria: the one situated 7 m. S of Tonneberg; and the other on the river Zeya, 5 m. E of Zistersdorf.
ABSON AND WICK, a parish and village in Gloucestershire, 7 m. E by N of Bristol. Acres 2,170. Pop. 794.
ABSTADT, a bailiwick and town of Würtemberg, in the barony of Lœwenstein-Rossenburg, circle of the Neckar, with 600 inhabitants.
ABSTEINACH, a small district and town of Baden, in the bailiwick of Starkenburg, 7 m. NNE of Heidelberg. Pop. 700.
ABSTETTEN, or AMSTETTEN, a market-town of Lower Austria, on the Ips, 26 m. ESE of Lintz. Pop. 900.
ABSWANGEN, a small town of East Prussia, in the bailiwick of Natangen, 12 m. S of Königsberg
ABTENAU, a town in the mountainous part of Saltzburg, at the confluence of the Arbenau and the Lamm, 20 m. SSE of Saltzburg.
ABTERODE, a town of Hessen, in the prov. or Cassel, 6 m. WNW of Eschwege. Pop. 972.
ABTHORPE, a parish and township in the co. of Northampton, 3 m. WSW from Towcester. Acres 1,960. Pop. 449.
ABTNAUNDORF, a town of Saxony, on the
ABTSAMAT, a v. in the Libyan desert, to the N of the Wad-el-Dakhel.
ABTSCHWIND, a market-town of Bavaria, in the tract called the Steigerwald, 10 m. E of Kitzingen.
ABTSDORF, a market-town of Bohemia, in the circle of Chrudim, SE of Leutomischl.—Also a town to the W of Stockerau.
ABTSGEMUND, a village of Würtemberg, at the junction of the Lein with the Kocher, 8 m. SW of Ellwangen. Pop. 613. There are iron-forges here.

ABU, a frequent prefix in African and Asiatic geography. It is the same word as the Arabic abu, i. e. father; and is written abo, abou, and abu indiscriminately. We have adopted the orthography Abu in this work, except in the case of the word Aboukir, which has perhaps obtained permanence with us.

ABU', a town of the Felsen-Tibboos, in the Sahara, SE of Teghery.-Also a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Ajmeer, near the source of the Banas. - Also a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Trebisond, on the coast of the Black sea, 25 m. SW of Gunieh, in lat. 41° 33' N.

ABU' (Mount), a celebrated mountain or range of mountains, of Northern India, in the prov. of Ajmeer, under N lat. 24° 40'. Its formation is gneiss, exhibiting in different parts massive veins of compact blue slate. From the loftiest of its peaks, called Gura Sikra, it is said the citadel of Jodpur has been discerned in a clear atmosphere. Colonel Tod in 1822 reached Mount Abú by the valley of the Bunas river, and has given an account of this mountain and its celebrated springs in his 'Travels in Western India.'

ABU'-AHMED, or ABU'-HAMED, a town of Nubia, on the l. bank of the Nile; in N lat. 19° 27', W long. 33° 34'. The caravans from Kordofan to Cairo used to pass this town, but now take the way of New Dongola.
ABU'-AIM, a town in the Fantee territory on the Gold coast of Africa, in N lat. 5° 20'.
ABU'-ARA', an affluent of the Rio Negro, falling into it on the N bank between Boavista and Castanheiro-novo.
ABU'-ARAS, a v. of Sennaar, at the junction of the Rohat with the Bahr-el-Azrek. Lat. 14° 30' N.

ABU'-ARISCH, a principality or petty state of Arabia, in the district of Yemen. It extends along the coast of the Red sea, from Loheia on the S, to the district of El Hejaz on the N, or from N lat. 15° 20' to 17° 40'. It is a narrow strip of arid and sterile land, receiving only a small supply of water in the streams which descend from the neighbouring mountains. Its principal product is dhourrah. The chief towns are Abú-Arisch, Jesan-Harrad, and Sabea. The chief of this little state bears the title of sherif.—The capital, of the same name, is 80 m. N of Loheia, and 24 m. from the sea; in N lat. 16° 40', E long. 42° 20'. Its pop. is about 4,500.
ABU'-AZEZ, or ABU'-AZEES, a town of Upper Egypt, on the l. bank of the Nile, 11 m. N of Assouan.

ABU'-BOURKA (RAS), a cape of Arabia, in the gulf of Akabah. The name signifies 'the Cape of the White veil;' and it is so called from a remarkable patch of light-coloured sand which renders it discernible from a great distance.—Wellsted.
ABUCARA, a Peruvian settlement, in the prov. of Lucanus, in N. lat. 15° 32'.
ABUCAY, a town on the NW coast of the island of Mindanao; N lat. 8° 40', E long. 125° 24'.
ABUCEES (SAN JOSE' DE LOS), a settlement of Indians, on a small river which enters the Putumayo, in N lat. 0° 10', W long. 74° 46'.
ABU'-CHAREB, a town of Sudan, on the confines of Darfur, 90 m. SE of Wara.

ABU'-CHEGER (JEBEL), a mountain of Upper ABU Egypt, in the prov. of Thebes, 36 m. S. of Edfu. Its name signifies the Mountain of Tempests, and has been given to it from the frequency with which clouds and storms appear to collect around it.
ABU'-CHIBUK, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Baghdad, on the r. or N bank of the Diyalah, in N lat. 33° 50′.
ABU'-DAKLU, a village of the Egyptian desert in the oasis of Dakkel, 9 m. SE of El-Kasr.
ABU'DE, a town of Nubia, on the E bank of the Nile, in N lat. 20° 33'.
ABU'-DELIF, a town of Persia, on the l. bank of the Tigris, 10 m. NNW of Samarrah.

ABU-DERA'J, a bold headland on the western coast of the gulf of Suez, to the S of the Wadi-Músa or Badiya. It is the eastern extremity of a lofty range of mountains which have not yet been explored by any modern traveller.
ABU'-EL-SHEIB, a town of Lower Egypt, 46 m. NE of Cairo.
ABU'-EL-MATAMAR, a town of Lower Egypt, 29 m. SE of Alexandria, near Lake Mareotis; in N lat 30° 53′, E long 30° 12′.
ABU'-FANEH, a town of Central Egypt, 18 m. S of Miniyeh.
ABU'-FAR, a town of Central Egypt, in the district of Ghizeh, 8 m. NW of Atfieh.
ABU'-FEIDE, a steep mountain on the r. bank of the Nile, opposite to Manfalut.
ABU'-FERNUN, the highest summit of the Belilyetah mountains, to the SW of Bona in Algiers.
ABU'GA, a river of Independent Tartary, flowing from the Algydun-Zano mountains into the Fovol, after a course of 180 m.
ABU'-GALGAL, a small stream in the pashalik of Aleppo, a tributary of the Euphrates.
ABU'-GANDER, a town of Central Egypt, in the district of Fayoom, 30 m. WNW of Benisouef.
ABU'-GHA'RA'T, a town between the Blue Nile and El-Obeid the capital of Kordofan.
ABU'-GHARIB, a town in the pashalik of Baghdad, near the l. bank of the Euphrates; N lat. 33° 14'.

ABU'-GI'RGEH, or ABU'-GIRG, a large Fellah town of Central Egypt, in the prov. of Ghibli, situated on a rich plain about 2 m. from the l. bank of the Nile, and 12 m. SSW of Behnesa. The pasha has established some cotton-spinning factories here, and an elementary school. Lat. 28° 32' N.
ABU'-HADID, a town of Kordofan, in N lat. 14° 33', near the iron-mines.
ABU'-HILAR, a town of Tafilet, 60 m. E of Sijilmesah.
ABU'-HILID, a town of Persia, in the pashalik of Bagdad, 20 m. E by N of Samarrah.
ABU'-HINAN, or ABU ANAN, a village, tr., and river of Marocco, E of Wad Ghir. Lat. 31° 40' N, long. 2° 30' W.—Carte de Maroc.
ABU'-HONNES, or DAYR-ABU'-HONNES [i. e. the Convent of Father John], a modern Christian village, in the prov. of Thebes in Upper Egypt.
ABU'-HOR, a district and town of Nubia, above the rapids of Kabolshieh. The country presents a pleasing scene of scattered hamlets, fields, and gardens.
ABU'-HOUAL, a town of Upper Egypt, on the r. bank of the Nile, 12 m. above Girgeh.
ABU'HURAZ, a town of Kordofan, in N lat. 13°, E long. 29°.
ABU'-HUSAN, a town of Al-Jezireh, on the r. bank of the Euphrates, in N lat. 35° 16'.

ABUJA, or AGUJA (CAPE), a headland on the N coast of Africa, in N lat. 35° 53', W long. 0° 29', 15 m. NE of Oran, in the prov. of Algiers. It presents a pile of barren rocks terminating in a peak 2,080 ft. above the sea. A mile to the eastward of it, at about a cable's length from the shore is a pyramidal rock, 177 ft. in height, known to European sailors as the Needle; by the natives called Seba Pharaon, i. e. Pharaoh's thumb. Cape Ferrat, which is sometimes confounded with Cape Abuja, lies 4 m. E of the Needle.
ABU'-JABBAR, a town in the pashalik of Aleppo, 35 m. E of Aleppo.
ABU'-JEHAARAT, a town of Sennár, in N lat. 13° 49′.
ABU'-JENUCH, a town of Sennár, on the r. bank of the Bahr-el-Abiad.
ABU'-KASHEID, a town of Lower Egypt, 58 m. NE of Cairo.

ABU'-KHABEES, or ABU'-KOBEIS, a mountain 5 m. SE of Mecca, greatly venerated by Mahommedans. "Here," says Hháji Khalífeh, "Adam was buried; hence Abraham called all the sons of men to perform the pilgrimage; and here the Prophet split the moon in half by waving his hand, in commemoration of which a grotto called Mahalli Shakku'l kamar, [i. e. the Place of the Splitting of the Moon,'] was formed near its summit."—Burckhardt, Arabia, p. 174.
ABU'-KHARRA'Z, a town on the r. bank of the Blue Nile, at the junction of the Rahad, coming from the E.—Holroyd.
ABU'KI'R. See ABOUKIR.
ABU'-KOH, a v. of Syria, on the route from Jaffa to Jerusalem; 12 m. NW of the latter city.
ABU'KOR, a kádilik and town, the sanjak of Aineh-bakhti in Romelia, on the Phidari, NW of Karavari.
